stories.title: Arthur Fery Stuns Dimitrov in Five Sets — First British Wild Card in Grand Slam QF (Open Era)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Arthur Fery defeats Grigor Dimitrov 7-5, 3-6, 4-6, 6-4, 7-6(10-7) to reach the Wimbledon quarterfinal. The 23-year-old wildcard is the first British wild card to reach a Grand Slam QF in the Open Era. Extraordinary. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: No British wild card has ever reached a Grand Slam quarterfinal in the Open Era. Arthur Fery just became the first — coming from two sets to one down to beat Dimitrov in a fifth-set tiebreak at Wimbledon. Up next: No. 9 Cobolli. #Wimbledon #Fery #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
HISTORY MADE AT WIMBLEDON.

Arthur Fery — 23 years old, ranked outside the top 100, playing on a wildcard — has done something no British wild card has ever done in the Open Era: reached a Grand Slam quarterfinal.

Fery defeated No. 28 Grigor Dimitrov 7-5, 3-6, 4-6, 6-4, 7-6(10-7) on Day 8 at Wimbledon, coming back from two sets to one down on Centre Court. At one point, Dimitrov needed just two service holds to close the match. He never got them.

Fery has been one of the most exciting young British players on the circuit, but nothing in his career to this point had suggested a result of this magnitude. He's also the first wildcard to reach the men's quarterfinals at Wimbledon since Nick Kyrgios in 2014.

His next opponent: No. 9 Flavio Cobolli — who just upset No. 5 Alex de Minaur on the same day. The fairytale continues on Wimbledon grass.

What a fortnight. What a story. Who do you think wins the Fery-Cobolli QF?
facebook.image_caption: Arthur Fery. 23 years old. Wild card. First British wildcard to reach a Grand Slam QF in Open Era history. Wimbledon 2026 is writing stories nobody expected.

article.headline: Arthur Fery Makes History: First British Wild Card to Reach a Grand Slam Quarterfinal in the Open Era

stories.title: Cobolli Eliminates No. 5 de Minaur + Day 8 Men's Results Complete

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: No. 9 Flavio Cobolli upsets No. 5 Alex de Minaur 7-5, 7-6(4), 6-3 at Wimbledon — the biggest men's seed upset of Day 8. Cobolli reaches his second consecutive Wimbledon quarterfinal. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: Taylor Fritz is into his third straight Wimbledon QF after beating Bublik 7-6(1), 6-4, 6-4. His QF opponent: the winner of Zverev vs Lehecka — suspended overnight with Zverev leading 6-4, 7-5, 3-3. Fritz is 7-0 vs Zverev all-time. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Day 8 at Wimbledon delivered the kind of drama that makes the grass-court major unlike anything else in tennis.

No. 9 Flavio Cobolli eliminated No. 5 Alex de Minaur 7-5, 7-6(4), 6-3 — the biggest men's seed upset of the day. De Minaur led by a break in both the second and third sets. Cobolli broke back each time. That resilience is why Cobolli is now in his second consecutive Wimbledon quarterfinal.

Taylor Fritz (No. 6) also advanced, defeating Alexander Bublik 7-6(1), 6-4, 6-4 for his third straight Wimbledon QF. His potential next opponent — Alexander Zverev — was suspended overnight at Wimbledon's 11 PM curfew, leading Jiri Lehecka 6-4, 7-5, 3-3 in the third set. The match resumes Day 9.

Fritz is 7-0 all-time against Zverev. That head-to-head matters if Zverev advances.

The men's draw is as wild as advertised. What do you make of it?
facebook.image_caption: No. 9 Cobolli eliminating No. 5 de Minaur. Fritz into his third straight Wimbledon QF. Zverev-Lehecka resumes today. Day 8 was wild — Day 9 QFs start now.

article.headline: Cobolli Upsets No. 5 de Minaur to Reach Second Straight Wimbledon Quarterfinal

stories.title: Eala's Wimbledon Run Ends — Women's R4 Results

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: Jasmine Paolini defeats Alexandra Eala 6-4, 4-6, 6-3, ending the historic Wimbledon run of the first Filipino Grand Slam second-week player in the Open Era. Paolini advances to her third major quarterfinal. #Wimbledon #WTA #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Her run is over. But what a run it was.

Alexandra Eala — the first Filipino player to reach the second week of a Grand Slam in the Open Era — fell to No. 13 Jasmine Paolini 6-4, 4-6, 6-3 on Day 8 at Wimbledon. Paolini was simply a step too far.

The rest of the women's quarterfinal field is now fully set after a busy Day 8. Linda Noskova (No. 9) defeated Madison Keys (No. 26) 6-4, 7-6(2) for her first-ever Wimbledon QF. Elise Mertens (No. 25) defeated Marie Bouzkova 6-4, 6-4 — also a first Wimbledon QF. Marta Kostyuk (No. 12) defeated Ashlyn Krueger 6-4, 6-4.

Here is what makes this women's draw remarkable: there is not a single past Wimbledon champion left in the field. Wimbledon 2026 is guaranteed to crown a first-time women's champion.

A new name goes on the trophy. Who do you think it will be?
facebook.image_caption: Eala's historic Wimbledon run ends — but the first Filipino Grand Slam second-week player in the Open Era leaves with her head held high. A first-time women's champion is coming to Wimbledon 2026.

article.headline: Eala's Historic Wimbledon Run Ends — Women's Quarterfinal Field Fully Set

stories.title: Wimbledon Day 9 QF Preview — FAA vs Djokovic, Sinner vs Struff, Gauff vs Pegula

x_posts.label: Post 4-1
x_posts.text: Coco Gauff vs Jessica Pegula at Wimbledon today — the first all-American women's QF between two top-10 seeds since Serena vs Venus in 2009. Pegula leads their H2H 5-3. Who wins Centre Court? #Wimbledon #WTA #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 4-2
x_posts.text: No. 3 Felix Auger-Aliassime vs No. 7 Novak Djokovic at Wimbledon today. FAA fighting for his first Grand Slam title. Djokovic fighting for his 25th major and 8th Wimbledon. H2H tied 1-1 — first match ever on grass. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Wimbledon quarterfinal day is here — and the lineup could not be more compelling.

On Centre Court, Coco Gauff (No. 7) faces Jessica Pegula (No. 4) in the first all-American women's quarterfinal between two top-10 seeds at Wimbledon since the 2009 Serena–Venus final. Pegula leads their head-to-head 5-3 overall, and 1-1 on grass.

Also on Centre Court: No. 3 Felix Auger-Aliassime against No. 7 Novak Djokovic. FAA carries momentum after a five-set battle in the round of 16. Djokovic, 39, is chasing his 25th Grand Slam title and eighth Wimbledon championship. Their head-to-head is tied 1-1 — but this is the first time they have ever played on grass.

On No. 1 Court (a scheduling decision that has drawn criticism): No. 1 Jannik Sinner, the defending champion, faces Jan-Lennard Struff. Sinner has not dropped a set all tournament. Then Naomi Osaka (No. 14) faces Karolina Muchova (No. 10) — a rematch of the Bad Homburg final just nine days ago, which Muchova won.

Four matches. All of them matter. Which one are you most excited for?
facebook.image_caption: FAA vs Djokovic. Gauff vs Pegula. Sinner vs Struff. Osaka vs Muchova. Wimbledon quarterfinal day is here — and the stakes could not be higher.

article.headline: Wimbledon Day 9: FAA vs Djokovic, Gauff vs Pegula Headline Star-Studded Quarterfinal Lineup

stories.title: Wimbledon QF Field — Underdogs, Upsets, and the Chaos of a Wide-Open Draw

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Felix Auger-Aliassime on Wimbledon's medical timeout rule: "A disgrace of a rule." His opponent called a physio while FAA served for the match at 5-4 — then won that set. FAA's proposed fix: forfeit all points in the game. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Felix Auger-Aliassime did not mince words after his Wimbledon round of 16 victory.

When his opponent, Alejandro Davidovich Fokina, called for a medical timeout while FAA was serving for the match at 5-4 in the fourth set, Fokina received treatment — and when he came back, he won the set. FAA won the fifth set 6-1 to close out the match, but what happened in the fourth stayed with him.

"A disgrace of a rule," FAA said afterward.

His proposed fix: any player who calls for a physio during their opponent's service game should forfeit every point in that game. The logic removes any tactical incentive without eliminating the right to receive treatment.

Andy Roddick said he personally would not have shown FAA's restraint.

The debate over medical timeouts in professional tennis has been ongoing for years. FAA's comments — specific, direct, and backed by a concrete proposal — have put it back at the center of the sport's biggest tournament.

Do you think the rule needs to change?
facebook.image_caption: "A disgrace of a rule." Felix Auger-Aliassime is calling out Wimbledon's medical timeout rules — and has a specific proposal to fix it. The debate is back.

article.headline: Felix Auger-Aliassime Calls Wimbledon's Medical Timeout Rule "A Disgrace" — and Proposes a Fix
